name: Southwick
overview: Southwick is a historic estate village beneath the northern slopes of Portsdown Hill in Hampshire, a few miles north of Portsmouth and Portchester. It is famous as the 'D-Day Village': from nearby Southwick House, General Eisenhower made the final decision to launch Operation Overlord in June 1944. Unusually, the village is still effectively a feudal estate, largely owned by one family (the Thistlethwaytes) since the 16th century, which has kept its mix of medieval, Georgian and Victorian cottages immaculate (all with the estate's trademark deep-red front doors).
character: An exceptionally well-preserved, estate-owned village around a green, rich in D-Day heritage and unusually generous in amenities for its small size.
amenities: Two pubs: the Golden Lion (WWII officers' mess) and the Red Lion (Grade II listed)
Southwick Brewhouse (Scheduled Ancient Monument, Victorian steam brewery)
Village green with the original water pump
Early medieval church and priory ruins

prices.summary: Much of the village is estate-owned, so sales are relatively rare; period and listed cottages are highly sought after when available. See Rightmove for current sold-price detail.

thingsToDo: D-Day Memorial Hall and Southwick House heritage
Walks to the priory ruins and Portsdown Hill
pros: Two characterful pubs, a shop/tea room and brewery
Quiet rural setting close to Portsmouth and Fareham
cons: Largely estate-owned, so few homes come to market
No school in the village itself
whoItSuits: Buyers drawn to a unique, heritage-rich estate village near Portsmouth, who value character and history over modern convenience.
